cards.text: To isolate a problem, you can {{c1::comment out}} sections of code and see if the issue {{c2::disappears}}, helping narrow down where the bug is located.

cards.front: What are defensive assumptions in programming?
cards.back: Validating that your assumptions are correct by adding checks (like assertions or print statements) to verify values are what you expect them to be.

cards.text: Instead of using interactive input during debugging, use {{c1::hardcoded}} test values so you can quickly {{c2::rerun}} the program with the same inputs.

cards.question: What does this debugging code do?
cards.answer: Conditionally compiles debug output. You can enable/disable all debug prints by defining or commenting out ENABLE_DEBUG.
cards.explanation: This allows you to leave debug code in place but easily turn it on/off.

cards.front: Why should you print both variable names and values when debugging?
cards.back:
Printing just values can be confusing. Including variable names makes output self-documenting and easier to understand.

Example: std::cout << "x = " << x;

cards.explanation:
When you don't know where a bug is:
  • Comment out half the code
  • If bug persists, it's in active half
  • If bug disappears, it's in commented half
  • Repeat, dividing the suspect region in half each time
examples: // Comment out lines 50-100 → still broken? Bug in 1-50
// Comment out lines 25-50 → fixed? Bug in 25-50

cards.text: When adding debug print statements, use a {{c1::prefix}} or {{c2::log level}} to distinguish them from normal program output.

cards.front: What's the advantage of using preprocessor directives for debug output?
cards.back: Debug code can be left in the source but easily enabled/disabled. When disabled, it's not compiled, so there's no runtime performance cost.

cards.question: Why might this debug statement not help you?
cards.answer: You can't tell which value is which. Should use: std::cout << "x=" << x << " y=" << y << " z=" << z;
cards.explanation: Always label your debug output so you know what you're looking at.

cards.front: Why use hardcoded test values instead of user input while debugging?
cards.back: Hardcoded values let you quickly rerun the program with identical inputs, making it easier to reproduce bugs and test fixes.

cards.text: Don't assume your code is {{c1::correct}} - validate your {{c2::assumptions}} by checking that variables contain expected values at key points.

cards.explanation:
Best practices for debug printing:
  • Print both variable names and values
  • Use a distinctive prefix (e.g., "DEBUG: ")
  • Use preprocessor directives to enable/disable
  • Print at key checkpoints (function entry/exit, before/after calculations)
  • Remove or disable before release

cards.text: The {{c1::binary search}} debugging technique involves commenting out {{c2::half}} the code repeatedly to narrow down the bug location.

cards.question: How do you conditionally enable all debug output?
cards.answer: Define ENABLE_DEBUG at the top. All #ifdef ENABLE_DEBUG blocks will be compiled. Comment out the #define to disable all debug output.
cards.explanation: This allows you to turn all debug code on/off with a single line.
